perturbation.original_text: 4.8. AUDITS. During the term of this Agreement and for one year thereafter, VerticalNet shall have the right to appoint a certified public accountant to audit Impresse's financial records relating to such payment to verify the accuracy of Impresse's financial records in order to verify the amount of the payments owed and/or paid hereunder, but no more frequently than once per year.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.8. AUDITS. During the term of this Agreement, VerticalNet shall have the right to appoint a certified public accountant to audit Impresse's financial records relating to such payment to verify the accuracy of Impresse's financial records in order to verify the amount of the payments owed and/or paid hereunder, and may do so up to four times per year.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the audit frequency to four times per year could be deemed unreasonable and overly burdensome, potentially violating implied covenants of good faith and fair dealing present in Delaware contract law. Delaware law requires parties to a contract to act in good faith.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing under Delaware Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification allows audits four times a year, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Delaware law, as such frequent audits could be viewed as an unreasonable burden on Impresse, disrupting their business operations and undermining the spirit of cooperation expected in a contractual relationship. The implied covenant requires each party to act in a way that does not frustrate the other party's right to receive the benefits of the agreement. While the original text allows audits, limiting them to once per year demonstrates a balance that the modified text removes.

(a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this State and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this State or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), the Uniform Commercial Code applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. The parties, by agreement, may determine the standards by which the performance of those obliga
perturbation.justification: The changed text increases the audit frequency from once per year to up to four times per year. This contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which Delaware law recognizes. While Snippet 1, Delaware Code Title 6, Â§ 1-302(b), states that "The obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness, and care prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement," it does not explicitly define the standards. However, the perturbation and law explanations both state that increasing the audit frequency to four times a year is an unreasonable burden. The original agreement allowed for audits, but limited them to once a year, showing balance. The increased frequency in the modified text is a contradiction because it could be considered an overreach, violating the implied covenant of good faith. The first snippet allows for variation by agreement except for good faith requirements which makes this agreement vulnerable to a good faith challenge.

perturbation.original_text: 6.5. TWO YEAR LIMITATION. Except for claims under Sections 9.4 [INDEMNIFICATION BY IMPRESSE] and 9.5 [INDEMNIFICATION BY VERTICALNET] Source: IMPRESSE CORP, S-1/A, 3/22/2000 hereof, neither party may bring a claim or action regardless of form, arising out of or related to this Agreement, including any claim of fraud or misrepresentation, more than two years after the cause of action accrues or becomes known, whichever is later.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.5. TWO YEAR LIMITATION. Except for claims under Sections 9.4 [INDEMNIFICATION BY IMPRESSE] and 9.5 [INDEMNIFICATION BY VERTICALNET] Source: IMPRESSE CORP, S-1/A, 3/22/2000 hereof, neither party may bring a claim or action regardless of form, arising out of or related to this Agreement, including any claim of fraud or misrepresentation, more than one year after the cause of action accrues or becomes known, whichever is later.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the limitation period for claims to one year could be problematic under Delaware law, especially for claims involving fraud or breach of contract where a longer statute of limitations might apply. It could be considered unconscionable if it unduly prejudices one party. Delaware law allows for a three-year statute of limitations for fraud claims.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Statute of Limitations for Fraud
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text reduces the limitation period to one year, directly contradicting Delaware's statute of limitations for fraud claims, which typically allows for a longer period (e.g., three years). This change could prevent a party from pursuing a valid fraud claim if the fraudulent activity is discovered more than one year after it occurred but within the statutory period of three years.

PERSONAL ACTIONS § 8106. Actions subject to 3-year limitation. (a) No action to recover damages for trespass, no action to regain possession of personal chattels, no action to recover damages for the detention of personal chattels, no action to recover a debt not evidenced by a record or by an instrument under seal, no action based on a detailed statement of the mutual demands in the nature of debit and credit between parties arising out of contractual or fiduciary relations, no action based on a promise, no action based on a statute, and no action to recover damages caused by an injury unaccompanied with force or resulting indirectly from the act of the defendant shall be brought after the expiration of 3 years from the accruing of the cause of such action; subject, however, to the provisions of §§ 8108-8110, 8119 and 8127 of this title. (b) Contractual limitations. — Notwithstanding any other provision of this section, a property insurance contract subject to subchapter III of Chapter 41 of Title 18: (1) May not require that an action for a claim made under the contract be filed less than 1 year from the date of the denial of the claim by the insurer; and (2) May permit an action for a claim made under the contract to be filed more than 1 year from the date of the denial of the claim by the insurer. (c) Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in this chapter (oth
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the limitation period for claims arising from the agreement from two years to one year. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this as potentially problematic, especially for fraud claims where a longer statute of limitations might apply under Delaware law. The Law Explanation states that this change could prevent a party from pursuing a valid fraud claim within the typical three-year statutory period. Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically 10 Delaware Code § 8106 (2023), indicates a three-year limitation for actions based on a statute. By reducing the limitation period to one year, the modified text directly contradicts the three-year limitation provided by Delaware law, creating a legal contradiction and potentially making the contractual term unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 4.9. INTEREST. All payments not paid by the date such payments are due shall bear interest from the due date to the date payments are actually paid at the lower of (i) 1% per month or (ii) the maximum rate permitted by law.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.9. INTEREST. All payments not paid by the date such payments are due shall bear interest from the due date to the date payments are actually paid at a rate of 25% per month.
perturbation.explanation: Setting the interest rate to 25% per month (300% per year) is usurious and violates usury laws, which exist to prevent predatory lending. Since the agreement is governed by Delaware law, we need to look at Delaware usury laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law sets limits on interest rates, and 25% per month significantly exceeds those limits in most scenarios, unless an exception applies (e.g., very large loans). By setting such a high rate, the modified text creates a usurious contract, rendering the interest provision unenforceable and potentially exposing the lender to penalties.
perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly sets an interest rate of 25% per month, while the original text limited it to the lower of 1% per month or the maximum rate permitted by law. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that this 25% rate likely violates usury laws. The lack of scraped law snippets does not negate the strong indication of a legal contradiction based on the provided explanations and the significant change in interest rate.
